APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it proposes a semi‑supervised method (TagLM) that augments sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings and evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 chunking). The experiments use venue‑expected methods and evaluations (multiple runs with seeds, F1 reporting, significance testing) and demonstrate new state‑of‑the‑art results (e.g., 91.93 mean F1 on CoNLL NER vs prior 91.62). The paper is well positioned with respect to related work on pretrained embeddings, neural LMs, and semi‑supervised sequence tagging, making it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clearly articulated new use of pretrained bidirectional language model states as token-level contextual embeddings (Section 2.3 and Fig.1) and shows how to plug these LM embeddings into supervised CRF-based taggers, trained on large unlabeled data (Section 3). The authors explicitly differentiate from prior work (e.g., Li and McCallum 2005 used generative latent variables; Peris & Casacuberta 2015 used tied bidirectional LMs) and emphasize their use of independent forward/backward LMs and concatenation of top-LSTM outputs as features. The idea is a non-trivial, practically impactful extension of existing transfer/representation methods rather than a purely incremental tweak, so it would be informative to experts even though it builds on known components.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a generally logical structure (model description, pre-trained LMs, experiments, related work) and key ideas (use of forward+backward LM embeddings) are explained (e.g., Sec. 2.3). However the provided text contains many clear presentation problems: repeated and duplicated paragraphs, truncated sentences, garbled table/figure references, and stray placeholders (e.g., “Test data set performance withheld...”, repeated blocks in Sect. 2 and 4) that interrupt flow and make parts hard to follow. Notation is mostly defined (LM probability and hLMk), but inconsistent formatting of symbols and occasional typographical errors (“more then”) reduce clarity.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are substantial: evaluation on two standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking) with comparisons to prior work, statistical significance testing, 10 random-seed runs with mean±std, and a low-resource (1% training) sensitivity check showing larger gains. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are present but limited — the paper quantifies the average F1 gain from LM embeddings and compares different pretrained LM sizes (CNN-BIG-LSTM and smaller LSTM-2048-512), yet it lacks explicit ablations such as forward-only vs. backward-only embeddings or detailed analysis of where/how embeddings contribute. Related work coverage is thorough, but discussion of failure cases or limitations is minimal and some reproduction details (exact integration points, full hyperparameter listings) are partial despite reporting major LM architectures and training setup.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important, widely applicable problem—improving sequence tagging by injecting pretrained bidirectional language-model embeddings—and demonstrates clear practical gains: TagLM achieves new state-of-the-art results (CoNLL 2003 NER 91.93 mean F1 vs prior 91.62; CoNLL 2000 chunking 96.37 vs prior 95.77) and reports average absolute improvements of ~1.06 and 1.37 F1. It also substantially helps low-resource settings (e.g., a 3.35% jump with 1% training data), and the approach is general (LM embeddings can be added to other discriminative models and tasks), making it likely to be widely adopted and to open significant follow-on research on pretrained contextual representations.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares TagLM to recent state-of-the-art systems (e.g., Chiu & Nichols 2016, Lample et al. 2016, Ma & Hovy 2016, Hashimoto et al. 2016, Yang et al. 2017) in Tables 1–4 and separates results with and without external resources/gazetteers. Comparisons appear fair (same CoNLL datasets, consistent preprocessing following Chiu & Nichols, reporting mean±std over 10 runs, and statistical significance tests), and the authors quantify improvements (absolute F1 gains, significance) and analyze effects (including low-resource 1% experiment). The paper also cites and discusses the most relevant competing approaches and related methods (LMs, semi‑supervised transfer, prior bidirectional LM work), so comparisons are comprehensive and well contextualized.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (adding pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings improves sequence tagging) and supports it with experiments on CoNLL NER and Chunking showing higher mean F1 and reported statistical tests (they train each configuration 10 times and report mean/std; NER improvement tested with a Welch t-test, p=0.021). The methodological description (pretraining on the 1B Word benchmark, CNN-BIG-LSTM and smaller LMs, extracting top-layer LM states, concatenating forward/backward embeddings, baseline comparisons) is sound. Minor issues remain: the chunking significance claim relies on an assumed standard deviation (“assuming standard deviation of 0.1”), and the paper does not explicitly state whether LM embeddings are fine-tuned or how capacity/parameter-count confounds were controlled for when adding LM features. These do not invalidate the main conclusions but are noteworthy methodological omissions.
